query: Towards the one exposome: harnessing artificial intelligence and digital health technologies across one health toxicology, new approach methodologies and environmental public health

t1.1.name: Multi-Domain Exposure Assessment Methods
t1.1.description: Approaches for measuring and characterizing exposures across internal, external, and general environmental domains including biomonitoring, personal sensors, and geospatial exposure modeling.

t1.name: Exposome Conceptual Framework and Integration
t1.description: The exposome paradigm encompasses total lifetime environmental exposures and their integration across biological, chemical, and social domains. This foundational concept requires integration of One Health principles linking human, animal, and environmental health.

t2.1.name: AI-Driven Toxicity Prediction and NAMs
t2.1.description: Application of AI models to new approach methodologies (NAMs) for toxicity testing, including in silico models, QSAR approaches, and machine learning-based alternatives to animal testing for chemical hazard assessment.

t2.name: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications
t2.description: AI and ML methods for processing high-dimensional exposome data, predicting toxicological outcomes, and integrating multi-omics datasets. Includes deep learning for exposure-disease associations and predictive toxicology models.

t3.name: Digital Health Technologies and Real-Time Monitoring
t3.description: Wearable devices, mobile health applications, and IoT sensors for continuous environmental and biological monitoring. Integration of digital health data streams for environmental public health surveillance and personalized exposure assessment.

t1.1.name: Total Exposome Characterization
t1.1.description: Comprehensive mapping of internal and external exposures including chemical, biological, physical, and social stressors across environmental media and biological compartments.

t1.2.name: One Health Toxicology Integration
t1.2.description: Application of toxicological principles across human, animal, and environmental health domains to assess shared vulnerability and cross-species extrapolation for chemical risk assessment.

t1.name: One Health and Exposome Framework
t1.description: Integration of One Health principles with exposome science, examining how environmental, animal, and human health interconnect through cumulative exposure assessment across all life stages and environmental domains.

t2.1.name: AI-Driven Predictive Toxicology
t2.1.description: Machine learning models for predicting toxicity endpoints, QSAR modeling, adverse outcome pathway prediction, and in silico hazard identification without animal testing.

t2.name: AI and Machine Learning Applications
t2.description: Artificial intelligence and computational methods for exposome data integration, predictive toxicology, pattern recognition in environmental health data, and automated analysis of complex exposure-response relationships.

t3.name: New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)
t3.description: Non-animal testing methods including in vitro assays, organ-on-chip systems, high-throughput screening, omics technologies, and computational approaches for chemical safety assessment and regulatory decision-making.

t4.name: Digital Health Technologies
t4.description: Wearable sensors, mobile health apps, real-time exposure monitoring devices, and digital epidemiology tools for continuous personal exposure assessment and health outcome tracking in population studies.

t1.1.name: One Health Integration in Exposome Research
t1.1.description: Integration of human, animal, and environmental health perspectives within exposome frameworks, emphasizing interconnected ecological systems and cross-species toxicological relevance.

t1.name: Exposome Paradigm and Conceptual Frameworks
t1.description: Foundational concepts of the exposome approach, including definitions, theoretical frameworks, and the evolution from traditional exposure assessment to comprehensive life-course environmental exposure characterization.

t2.1.name: AI-Driven Exposure Assessment and Modeling
t2.1.description: Use of artificial intelligence for real-time exposure monitoring, geospatial modeling of environmental hazards, and predictive exposure assessment across populations.

t2.2.name: Machine Learning in Toxicological Data Analysis
t2.2.description: Application of ML algorithms to analyze toxicological endpoints, predict adverse outcomes, and identify complex exposure-response relationships in high-dimensional datasets.

t2.name: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications
t2.description: Application of AI, machine learning, and computational methods to exposome research, including predictive modeling, pattern recognition in exposure data, and integration of multi-omic datasets.

t3.1.name: Sensor Networks and IoT for Environmental Monitoring
t3.1.description: Deployment of interconnected sensor networks and IoT devices for high-resolution spatial and temporal monitoring of environmental contaminants and exposures.

t3.name: Digital Health Technologies and Personal Exposure Monitoring
t3.description: Integration of wearable sensors, mobile health applications, and digital biomarking technologies for continuous, personalized exposure assessment and health outcome tracking.

t4.1.name: Integration of NAMs with AI for Risk Assessment
t4.1.description: Combining new approach methodologies with artificial intelligence to enhance toxicological risk assessment, reduce animal testing, and improve predictive accuracy for human health outcomes.

t4.name: New Approach Methodologies in Toxicology
t4.description: Non-animal testing methods including in vitro assays, organ-on-chip systems, computational toxicology, and high-throughput screening approaches that align with 3Rs principles.

t5.name: Data Integration and Interoperability Challenges
t5.description: Technical and methodological challenges in integrating heterogeneous data sources, standardizing exposome data, and ensuring interoperability across different monitoring platforms and databases.

t1.1.name: One Health Toxicology Principles
t1.1.description: Integration of human, animal, and environmental health perspectives in toxicological assessment, recognizing interconnected exposure pathways and shared environmental determinants.

t1.2.name: Exposure Assessment Methodologies
t1.2.description: Methods for measuring and characterizing environmental, occupational, and lifestyle exposures including biomonitoring, personal sensors, and exposure modeling approaches.

t1.name: Exposome Conceptual Framework
t1.description: Foundational concepts defining the exposome as the totality of environmental exposures across the lifespan, integrating internal and external exposure domains within the One Health paradigm.

t2.1.name: Predictive Toxicology Models
t2.1.description: AI-based computational models for predicting chemical toxicity, adverse outcome pathways, and dose-response relationships without animal testing.

t2.2.description: AI techniques for integrating genomics, metabolomics, proteomics, and exposomics data to identify exposure-disease relationships and biological mechanisms.

t2.description: Machine learning, deep learning, and AI-driven approaches for exposure prediction, toxicity modeling, risk assessment, and integration of multi-omics and environmental data.

t4.1.description: Cell-based assays, tissue models, and microphysiological systems that replicate human biological responses to environmental and chemical exposures.

t4.2.description: Automated platforms for rapid testing of large chemical libraries using standardized assays to identify hazards and prioritize substances for further evaluation.

t4.1.description: Cell-based assays and high-throughput screening platforms for rapid evaluation of chemical toxicity, including reporter gene assays and phenotypic screening approaches.
